MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT On Behalf Of Defendant Alireza Rey Alavi, D.O.Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. 437c
Off Calendar Oct-27-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: Defendant's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT On Behalf Of Defendant Alireza Rey Alavi, D.O.Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. 437c.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Dr. Alireza Alavi's motion for summary judgment is denied. On a summary judgment motion, the court strictly construes the moving party's evidence and liberally construes the opposing evidence. (See Stratton v. First National Life Insurance Co. (1989) 210 Cal.App.3d 1071, 1083.) In opposition, plaintiff presents expert evidence that Alavi caused plaintiff's injuries. (Response to UMFs 27; Declarations of Deborah Gold, M.D. and Lance Downing, M.D.) "A party may move for summary judgment in an action or proceeding if it is contended that the action has no merit or that there is no defense to the action or proceeding." (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 437c, subd. (a).) "A cause of action has no merit if either of the following exists: (1) [o]ne or more of the elements of the cause of action cannot be separately established, even is that element is separately pleaded[; or] (2) [a] defendant establishes an affirmative defense to that cause of action." (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 437c, subd. (o).) The moving party must make an initial showing, based upon affirmative evidence, that summary judgment must be granted. (See Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 437c, subd. (p); see Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Company (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 850.) A moving party may not simply claim plaintiff cannot prove all of the elements of his case, and challenge plaintiff to prove his case by opposition. (Y.K.A. Industries, Inc. v. Redevelopment Agency of City of San Jose (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 339, 353.) The moving party bears the burden of persuasion "from the commencement to conclusion" to show "that there is no triable issue of material fact and that [it] is entitled to judgment as a matter of law." (Aguilar, supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 850.) The court liberally construes the evidence in support of the party opposing summary judgment and resolves doubts concerning the evidence in favor of that party. (Hughes v. Pair (2009) 46 Cal.4th 1035, 1039.) When a defendant moves for summary judgment and supports his motion with an expert declaration that his conduct did not cause the plaintiff's injuries, the defendant is entitled to summary judgment unless the plaintiff comes forward with conflicting expert evidence. (See Salasguevara v. Wyeth Laboratories, Inc. (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 379 [medical causation is determined by expert testimony].) Here, Alavi argues he is entitled to summary judgement because none of his allegedly negligent conduct caused plaintiff Michael Bickar's injuries. Alavi maintained his initial burden of production on the issue of causation. The declaration of Patrick Joseph, M.D., shows that none of the alleged grounds for criticism of Alavi caused or contributed to plaintiff's injuries. (Defendant's Evidence, Vol. 4, Ex. E, pp. 12-13.) Plaintiff, however, has presented expert evidence that Alavi caused plaintiff's injuries. (Response to UMFs 27; Declarations of Deborah Gold, M.D. and Lance Downing, M.D.) Thus, there are triable issues of fact regarding causation. The motion, therefore, must be denied.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Joseph Quinn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Oct-18-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SUTTER LAKESIDE HOSPITAL'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Defendant's motion for summary judgment is denied.

On a summary judgment motion, the court strictly construes the moving party's evidence and liberally construes the opposing evidence. (See Stratton v. First National Life Insurance Co. (1989) 210 Cal.App.3d 1071, 1083.)

Defendant maintained its initial burden of production on the issues of breach and causation. (See CCP 437c(p)(2).) The declaration of Lakeisha N. Falwell, RN, NP, AOCNP, shows that defendant's employees and/or claimed agents complied with the standard of care and their conduct was not a substantial factor in causing injury to plaintiff. (Defendant's Evidence, Ex. 3; see also Ex. 2 [Johnson-Wicks Decl. re lack of agency with the physicians].)

When a defendant moves for summary judgment and supports his motion with an expert declaration that his conduct fell within the standard of care, the defendant is entitled to summary judgment unless the plaintiff comes forward with conflicting expert evidence. (See Munro v. Regents of the University of California (1989) 215 Cal.App.3d 977.)

In opposition, plaintiff presents expert evidence that defendant's employees and/or claimed agents violated the standard of care and caused plaintiff's injuries. (Response to UMFs 22-24, 26; Declarations of Deborah Gold, MD, Lance Downing, MD, Amanda Parrott, NP, and Jilly Urmy, RN, MSN.) There exists triable issues of material fact regarding breach and causation and the motion is therefore denied.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)
